stage.name: CVA6 architecture map: from IP boundary to pipeline spine
stage.description: A source-grounded OpenHW SlideKit microcourse that teaches CVA6 scope, module map, pipeline reading path, issue/scoreboard behavior, and frontend branch-prediction nuance.
stage.courseTemplateName: Professional Technical Deep Dive - Evidence-Grounded SlideKit

scenes.title: CVA6 architecture map: start from the boundary

content.title: CVA6 architecture map: from IP boundary to pipeline spine
content.subtitle: Use the official scope diagram and CV32A60X design document to build a practical source-reading map.
content.thesis: This is not generic CPU sightseeing. The question is practical: when you read CVA6 for the first time, how should official diagrams and design documents guide your route?

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 README architecture claim
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: CVA6 is positioned as a configurable 6-stage, single-issue, in-order RISC-V CPU with separate TLBs, hardware PTW, and branch prediction.

sourceAnchors.title: Scope of the CVA6 IP
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The documented IP comprises the CVA6 core, L1 write-through cache, optional FPU/MMU/PMP, CSR, performance counters, AXI interface, P-Mesh interface, and CV-X-IF; CLINT/PLIC, debug module, and L1 write-back cache support are outside the IP.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X subsystem and pipeline
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: CV32A60X is described as a Harvard-based processor where instructions are issued in-order through decode, executed out-of-order, committed in-order, and single issue means at most one instruction per cycle can be issued to execute.

content.speakerNotes: In this lesson we read CVA6 in a more engineering-oriented way. The goal is not to say that CVA6 is impressive; the goal is to split the material into a map we can actually use. We start with the IP boundary, then move to the architecture overview, and only after that look at Issue, Scoreboard, hazard behavior, and frontend branch prediction. The map keeps the technical discussion grounded.

actions.text: In this lesson we read CVA6 in a more engineering-oriented way. The goal is not to say that CVA6 is impressive; the goal is to split the material into a map we can actually use. We start with the IP boundary, then move to the architecture overview, and only after that look at Issue, Scoreboard, hazard behavior, and frontend branch prediction. The map keeps the technical discussion grounded.

content.title: First separate the evidence sources
content.subtitle: Official pages answer different questions; do not flatten them into one generic document.

cards.label: Positioning
cards.title: README
cards.body: Confirms high-level labels: six-stage pipeline, single-issue, in-order, plus TLB, PTW, and branch prediction clues.

cards.label: Usage boundary
cards.title: User Manual
cards.body: Explains audiences, the IP scope, and which system blocks are outside this IP.

cards.label: Internal structure
cards.title: Design Document
cards.body: Moves into CV32A60X pipeline, modules, scoreboard, hazards, and branch-prediction details.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 README architecture claim
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: CVA6 is positioned as a configurable 6-stage, single-issue, in-order RISC-V CPU with separate TLBs, hardware PTW, and branch prediction.

sourceAnchors.title: User Manual target audience
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The user manual targets SW programmers, HW integrators, coprocessor architects, implementation engineers, and verification engineers, while the design document covers internal microarchitecture.

sourceAnchors.title: Configuration-specific design documentation
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: Configuration-specific design documentation describes the internal architecture of the CVA6 processor and is generated from documentation inputs, some of which are directly generated from RTL.

content.speakerNotes: This slide is the quality-control layer. We do not let the classroom invent hardware claims. The README is responsible for official positioning. The User Manual explains scope and usage. The CV32A60X design document is where we should look for internal microarchitecture details such as pipeline stages, scoreboard behavior, hazards, and frontend prediction. Different source types answer different questions.

actions.text: This slide is the quality-control layer. We do not let the classroom invent hardware claims. The README is responsible for official positioning. The User Manual explains scope and usage. The CV32A60X design document is where we should look for internal microarchitecture details such as pipeline stages, scoreboard behavior, hazards, and frontend prediction. Different source types answer different questions.

scenes.title: Scope diagram: CVA6 is not a complete SoC

content.title: Scope diagram: CVA6 is not a complete SoC
content.body: First answer which layer you are studying: core/IP, interface, peripheral, and verification environment should not be mixed.
content.caption: CVA6 IP scope: core, cache, optional units, CSR/counters, and interfaces.

callouts.title: Inside the IP
callouts.body: Core, L1 write-through cache, optional FPU/MMU/PMP, CSR, counters, AXI, and CV-X-IF.

callouts.title: Outside the IP
callouts.body: CLINT, PLIC, debug module, and L1 write-back cache support are not inside this IP.

callouts.title: Learning value
callouts.body: A clear boundary prevents SoC-integration questions from being misread as core microarchitecture.

assets.caption: Official CVA6 scope diagram from the user manual archive.

sourceAnchors.title: Scope of the CVA6 IP
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The documented IP comprises the CVA6 core, L1 write-through cache, optional FPU/MMU/PMP, CSR, performance counters, AXI interface, P-Mesh interface, and CV-X-IF; CLINT/PLIC, debug module, and L1 write-back cache support are outside the IP.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 overview and configurable family
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: CVA6 is a RISC-V compatible application processor core configurable as CV32A6 or CV64A6, with optional MMU, PMP, FPU, cache organization and size, and more than 50 parameters.

content.speakerNotes: The first diagram deserves attention. The official material is clear about the scope: inside the CVA6 IP we see the core, L1 write-through cache, optional FPU, MMU, PMP, CSR, performance counters, and interfaces such as AXI and CV-X-IF. Outside that boundary sit blocks such as CLINT, PLIC, debug module, and some cache-support concerns. This boundary is the first guardrail for reading architecture correctly.

actions.text: The first diagram deserves attention. The official material is clear about the scope: inside the CVA6 IP we see the core, L1 write-through cache, optional FPU, MMU, PMP, CSR, performance counters, and interfaces such as AXI and CV-X-IF. Outside that boundary sit blocks such as CLINT, PLIC, debug module, and some cache-support concerns. This boundary is the first guardrail for reading architecture correctly.

scenes.title: Overview diagram: read the main path, not just module names

content.title: Overview diagram: read the main path, not just module names
content.body: The design document organizes the pipeline spine around FRONTEND, DECODE, ISSUE, EXECUTE, COMMIT, and CONTROLLER.
content.caption: CVA6/Ariane overview diagram as a reading map rather than a decorative architecture picture.

callouts.title: Fetch to decode
callouts.body: FRONTEND handles PC generation and fetch; DECODE turns instructions into control structures.

callouts.title: Issue to execute
callouts.body: ISSUE checks dependencies and resources; EX_STAGE contains ALU, branch, CSR, multiply, LSU, and CVXIF paths.

callouts.title: Commit state
callouts.body: COMMIT is where architectural state truly changes and precise exceptions land.

assets.caption: CVA6/Ariane high-level architecture diagram used as the first reading map.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 README architecture claim
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: CVA6 is positioned as a configurable 6-stage, single-issue, in-order RISC-V CPU with separate TLBs, hardware PTW, and branch prediction.

sourceAnchors.title: Architecture and module map
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The design document names FRONTEND, DECODE, ISSUE, EXECUTE, COMMIT and CONTROLLER as part of the pipeline, with CSRFILE containing registers.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X subsystem and pipeline
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: CV32A60X is described as a Harvard-based processor where instructions are issued in-order through decode, executed out-of-order, committed in-order, and single issue means at most one instruction per cycle can be issued to execute.

content.speakerNotes: Do not treat the overview as a poster. The design document gives us a pipeline reading spine: FRONTEND, DECODE, ISSUE, EXECUTE, COMMIT, and CONTROLLER. A useful reading question is not, can I memorize every box? A better question is, how does an instruction move from PC generation and fetch, through decode and issue, into execution, and finally into architectural state at commit?

actions.text: Do not treat the overview as a poster. The design document gives us a pipeline reading spine: FRONTEND, DECODE, ISSUE, EXECUTE, COMMIT, and CONTROLLER. A useful reading question is not, can I memorize every box? A better question is, how does an instruction move from PC generation and fetch, through decode and issue, into execution, and finally into architectural state at commit?

content.title: One instruction reading path
content.subtitle: The easily misunderstood sentence is: issue in-order, execute out-of-order, commit in-order.

steps.title: PC + Fetch
steps.body: Choose the PC, fetch a 32-bit block, and realign instructions.

steps.title: Decode
steps.body: Handle compressed instructions and create a scoreboard entry.

steps.title: Issue
steps.body: Check dependencies, functional units, and result-bus readiness.

steps.title: Execute
steps.body: ALU, branch, CSR, multiply, LSU, and CVXIF paths produce results.

steps.title: Commit
steps.body: Update architectural state in program order and handle stores, CSR, and exceptions.

content.takeaway: Single issue does not mean every event completes in a simple queue. Issue order, result return order, and commit order are three different concepts.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X subsystem and pipeline
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: CV32A60X is described as a Harvard-based processor where instructions are issued in-order through decode, executed out-of-order, committed in-order, and single issue means at most one instruction per cycle can be issued to execute.

sourceAnchors.title: Architecture and module map
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The design document names FRONTEND, DECODE, ISSUE, EXECUTE, COMMIT and CONTROLLER as part of the pipeline, with CSRFILE containing registers.

sourceAnchors.title: Execute and commit responsibilities
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: EX_STAGE encapsulates ALU, Branch Unit, CSR buffer, Mult, load/store, and CVXIF; COMMIT_STAGE updates architectural state, commits stores, writes GPR/CSR data, controls flush/stall, and handles precise exceptions at commit.

content.speakerNotes: Here is the first technical spine. The CV32A60X document describes a Harvard-based architecture where instructions issue in order, execution results may return out of order, and final commit happens in order. That sounds subtle because it is subtle. Single issue limits how instructions enter execution, but different functional units may finish at different times. The design still needs an in-order commit point to preserve architectural behavior.

actions.text: Here is the first technical spine. The CV32A60X document describes a Harvard-based architecture where instructions issue in order, execution results may return out of order, and final commit happens in order. That sounds subtle because it is subtle. Single issue limits how instructions enter execution, but different functional units may finish at different times. The design still needs an in-order commit point to preserve architectural behavior.

scenes.title: Scoreboard: the ledger for reordered results

content.title: Scoreboard: the ledger for reordered results
content.body: Issue is not only a launcher. It registers instructions, receives execution results, and hands completed entries to commit.
content.caption: Scoreboard sits between issue, execution result buses, and commit.

callouts.title: FIFO records
callouts.body: Each issued instruction occupies an entry and is removed only after commit. CV32A60X uses four entries.

callouts.title: Result fill-in
callouts.body: EX_STAGE results return by transaction ID and update the matching entry, with exception state captured too.

callouts.title: Commit outlet
callouts.body: The oldest completed entry is passed to COMMIT_STAGE so architectural state changes in order.

assets.caption: Scoreboard diagram from the CVA6 user manual asset archive.

sourceAnchors.title: ISSUE_STAGE and Scoreboard
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: ISSUE_STAGE issues instructions in-order, stores out-of-order results in Scoreboard, and sends completed instructions in-order to COMMIT_STAGE; the Scoreboard FIFO has 4 entries in this configuration.

sourceAnchors.title: Hazards and forwarding in issue_read_operands
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: Issue_read_operands tracks data and structural hazards, blocks RAW/WAW cases when needed, uses operand forwarding when legal, and stalls following instructions because issue is in-order.

content.speakerNotes: Scoreboard is the module to remember from this lesson. The document describes it as a FIFO: every issued instruction occupies an entry, and the entry is removed after commit. Execution results come back with a transaction ID and fill the matching entry, including exception information. Then the oldest completed entry can move to the commit stage. That is how out-of-order result return is reconciled with in-order architectural commit.

actions.text: Scoreboard is the module to remember from this lesson. The document describes it as a FIFO: every issued instruction occupies an entry, and the entry is removed after commit. Execution results come back with a transaction ID and fill the matching entry, including exception information. Then the oldest completed entry can move to the commit stage. That is how out-of-order result return is reconciled with in-order architectural commit.

scenes.title: Issue_read_operands: instructions do not issue just because they arrived

content.title: Issue_read_operands: instructions do not issue just because they arrived
content.subtitle: Separate the brakes from the bypasses in issue-stage behavior.

columns.label: Data hazard
columns.title: RAW / WAW handling
bullets: RAW may block issue when a source register waits for an unavailable destination in the Scoreboard.
When legal, operand forwarding can take values from functional-unit results or Scoreboard entries.
WAW blocks when the new instruction and an existing Scoreboard entry write the same destination.
x0 is a special case: the document explicitly says there is no x0 data hazard.

columns.label: Structural hazard
columns.title: Functional units and result bus can also stall
bullets: Issue checks whether the functional unit and result bus are ready.
Unknown-latency division or some CSR cases can block shared result-bus users.
Multiplication is fixed two cycles, but the multiplier is pipelined and can accept one instruction each cycle.
LSU users must wait for LSU readiness; in-order issue means later instructions cannot casually bypass.

anchor.label: Engineering cue
anchor.text: With in-order issue, if the current instruction stalls on a hazard, following instructions cannot simply run around it.

sourceAnchors.title: Hazards and forwarding in issue_read_operands
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: Issue_read_operands tracks data and structural hazards, blocks RAW/WAW cases when needed, uses operand forwarding when legal, and stalls following instructions because issue is in-order.

sourceAnchors.title: ISSUE_STAGE and Scoreboard
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: ISSUE_STAGE issues instructions in-order, stores out-of-order results in Scoreboard, and sends completed instructions in-order to COMMIT_STAGE; the Scoreboard FIFO has 4 entries in this configuration.

content.speakerNotes: Now let us explain why issue may stall. Issue_read_operands mainly checks data hazards and structural hazards. RAW and WAW relationships can block a new instruction, unless forwarding is legal and the value is available. Functional units and result buses also matter: a divider, a CSR path, or the LSU can stop progress. The practical lesson is that in-order issue has a visible cost: when the current instruction is blocked, following instructions cannot just overtake it.

actions.text: Now let us explain why issue may stall. Issue_read_operands mainly checks data hazards and structural hazards. RAW and WAW relationships can block a new instruction, unless forwarding is legal and the value is available. Functional units and result buses also matter: a divider, a CSR path, or the LSU can stop progress. The practical lesson is that in-order issue has a visible cost: when the current instruction is blocked, following instructions cannot just overtake it.

scenes.title: Frontend: the next PC is not always PC plus four

content.title: Frontend: the next PC is not always PC plus four
content.body: PC generation chooses among reset, prediction, default fetch, replay, exception, return, and commit PC sources.
content.caption: Branch prediction is taught here as frontend control logic, not as a generic CPU buzzword.

callouts.title: RAS
callouts.body: Return-style JALR can use a return address stack. CV32A60X uses RAS depth 2.

callouts.title: BHT
callouts.body: Conditional branches use a branch history table. This configuration has 32 BHT entries.

callouts.title: BTB nuance
callouts.body: The README mentions BTB/BHT, while the CV32A60X design document says this configuration has no BTB.

assets.caption: Branch prediction diagram from the CVA6 user manual asset archive.

sourceAnchors.title: FRONTEND PC generation and fetch
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: FRONTEND implements PC gen and Fetch stages; PC gen chooses next PC from reset, branch prediction, default fetch, replay, return, exception/interrupt, or commit PC; Fetch realigns data and feeds the instruction queue.

sourceAnchors.title: BHT, BTB and RAS behavior
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The frontend uses predecode plus RAS/BHT/BTB rules for branch prediction; in CV32A60X the BHT has 32 entries, RAS has 2 entries, and there is no BTB.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 README architecture claim
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: CVA6 is positioned as a configurable 6-stage, single-issue, in-order RISC-V CPU with separate TLBs, hardware PTW, and branch prediction.

content.speakerNotes: Finally, look at one frontend detail: the next PC is not always PC plus four. PC generation can choose among reset, branch prediction, default fetching, replay, exception or interrupt behavior, return paths, and commit PC. The branch-prediction discussion also teaches source discipline. A high-level README may mention BTB and BHT, while the CV32A60X design document says this configuration has no BTB. The variant context matters again.

actions.text: Finally, look at one frontend detail: the next PC is not always PC plus four. PC generation can choose among reset, branch prediction, default fetching, replay, exception or interrupt behavior, return paths, and commit PC. The branch-prediction discussion also teaches source discipline. A high-level README may mention BTB and BHT, while the CV32A60X design document says this configuration has no BTB. The variant context matters again.

scenes.title: Checkpoint: did you actually read the map?

questions.question: According to the User Manual scope of the IP, which group most accurately belongs inside CVA6 IP scope?

options.label: CVA6 core, L1 write-through cache, optional FPU/MMU/PMP, CSR, AXI, and CV-X-IF
CLINT, PLIC, debug module, and L1 write-back cache support
A complete SoC, all peripherals, board boot flow, and operating-system image
Only RISC-V ISA tables, with no interfaces or cache-related content

questions.analysis: The scope page places core, L1 write-through cache, optional FPU/MMU/PMP, CSR, performance counters, AXI, P-Mesh, and CV-X-IF inside the IP, while excluding blocks such as CLINT, PLIC, and debug module.

questions.question: Which statement best matches the CV32A60X design document pipeline behavior?

options.label: Instructions issue in order, execution results may return out of order, and final commit is in order
All functional units complete in one cycle, so no Scoreboard is needed
Single issue means several instructions can be issued to multiple execute ports in the same cycle
The commit stage does not change architectural state; it only prints debug information

questions.analysis: The document states that single issue means at most one instruction is issued to EXECUTE per cycle, while ISSUE_STAGE reorders execution results and passes the oldest completed instruction to COMMIT_STAGE in order.

questions.question: Which claims about CV32A60X Issue/Scoreboard and frontend are source-backed?

options.label: The Scoreboard FIFO has four entries in this configuration
RAW hazards may be mitigated with operand forwarding when conditions allow it
The CV32A60X design document says this configuration has no BTB
RAS must be flushed every time, otherwise commit cannot work

questions.analysis: Scoreboard capacity, RAW forwarding, and the no-BTB note come from the design document. The RAS section says the RAS is never flushed, so D is wrong.

actions.text: This checkpoint is about engineering reading, not memory tricks. The questions check whether you can keep the CVA6 IP boundary separate from SoC peripherals, explain the issue-result-commit relationship, and identify source-backed facts about Scoreboard, forwarding, and frontend prediction. If those three ideas are clear, you have a usable first architecture map.

scenes.title: Summary: use this map before reading RTL

content.title: Use this map before reading RTL
takeaways: The scope diagram separates IP internals from SoC-level peripherals.
The overview diagram creates a PC Generation to Commit reading spine.
Issue and Scoreboard connect out-of-order result return with in-order commit.
High-level README claims must be aligned with the configuration-specific design document.
content.nextStep: Next lesson: FRONTEND - PC generation, instr_queue, BHT/RAS, and mispredict behavior.
content.closing: If you remember one thing: build the map before reading RTL; read evidence before making claims.

sourceAnchors.title: Configuration-specific design documentation
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: Configuration-specific design documentation describes the internal architecture of the CVA6 processor and is generated from documentation inputs, some of which are directly generated from RTL.

sourceAnchors.title: Architecture and module map
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The design document names FRONTEND, DECODE, ISSUE, EXECUTE, COMMIT and CONTROLLER as part of the pipeline, with CSRFILE containing registers.

sourceAnchors.title: ISSUE_STAGE and Scoreboard
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: ISSUE_STAGE issues instructions in-order, stores out-of-order results in Scoreboard, and sends completed instructions in-order to COMMIT_STAGE; the Scoreboard FIFO has 4 entries in this configuration.

content.speakerNotes: Let us close the lesson. We did not cover all of CVA6, but we now have a map that works. Scope defines the boundary. Overview defines the pipeline spine. Issue and Scoreboard explain why a single-issue core can still have out-of-order result return and in-order commit. Frontend prediction reminds us that configuration-specific documents matter. With this map, the RTL directory becomes a route, not a pile.
